food_name: Whole Green Mung Beans, Raw Dried (with Skin)

local_names.romanized_local: sabut moong (Hindi); kacang hijau (Malay); pasi payaru (Tamil)

food_subgroup: Mung beans (Vigna radiata), whole, dried raw

ontology.foodex2_description: Mung bean (Vigna radiata), whole, dried raw
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for whole dried mung bean as a food item in available reference; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.description: 50 g dry (approx. 1 standard serving as dry ingredient)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of whole dried mung beans is approximately 50 g (about 3–4 tablespoons dry), which expands to roughly 100–120 g when cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Whole Green Mung Beans — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F09020021; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175188, Mung beans, mature seeds, raw) and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F09020021 is an exact match for whole green mung beans, raw dried with skin. Values cross-checked against USDA FDC ID 175188 (mung beans, mature seeds, raw) and ICMR-NIN 2017 table for sabut moong. All three sources are in close agreement. HPB values used as primary; USDA and ICMR-NIN used for cross-validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; boiled whole mung beans (Vigna radiata), GI approximately 31
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 31 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled whole mung beans (Vigna radiata). This GI applies to the cooked form; the dry raw weight is used as the nutritional reference basis. Glycemic load per 50 g dry serving calculated as: (31 × 62.6 × 0.50) / 100 = 9.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (1246 mg/100 g) and magnesium (189 mg/100 g) with very low sodium (15 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Whole mung beans are one of the most potassium-dense whole foods available and are strongly suitable for individuals with hypertension; their very high potassium-to-sodium ratio supports vascular tone and blood pressure regulation, and regular consumption as part of a DASH-aligned diet is clinically appropriate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~31 when boiled) combined with high dietary fibre (16.3 g/100 g) and high protein (23.9 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole mung beans have a very low GI of approximately 31 when boiled, and their high fibre and protein content further attenuates postprandial glucose response, making them an excellent carbohydrate source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the intact seed coat provides additional fibre compared to split moong dal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (16.3 g/100 g), and high folate (625 mcg DFE/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Whole mung beans are cholesterol-free, very low in saturated fat, and rich in soluble dietary fibre which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; their exceptionally high folate content (625 mcg DFE/100 g) also supports homocysteine metabolism, making them a cardioprotective legume suitable for regular inclusion in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate (625 mcg DFE/100 g), magnesium (189 mg/100 g), and iron (6.7 mg/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Whole mung beans are exceptionally high in folate, magnesium, and iron, nutrients relevant to inflammatory and haematological aspects of rheumatoid arthritis management; they are a foundational plant protein in traditional South and Southeast Asian dietary medicine for joint health and are appropriate for regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Whole green mung beans (Vigna radiata) are small, oval, olive-green dried legumes with the outer seed coat (skin) intact. They are distinct from split yellow moong dal, which has the hull removed and is pale yellow in colour. The whole bean retains more dietary fibre (16.3 g/100 g dry) due to the intact skin. Raw dried mung beans have a firm texture and a mild, earthy, beany aroma. When cooked, they become tender with a slightly sweet, nutty flavour. They can be prepared in multiple ways: boiled whole for sweet Chinese green bean soup (lǜ dòu tāng / tong sui), cooked savoury as Indian dal or curry (sabut moong dal), sprouted to produce fresh bean sprouts, or added to congee and rice porridge. Soaking for 1–2 hours is sufficient before cooking; prolonged soaking is not required. Nutritionally, whole mung beans are outstanding: extremely high in potassium (1246 mg/100 g), magnesium (189 mg/100 g), and folate (625 mcg DFE/100 g), with substantial protein (23.9 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (16.3 g/100 g). The glycemic index when cooked is approximately 31 (very low), making them highly suitable for blood glucose management. They are naturally cholesterol-free and very low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), supporting cardiovascular health. Widely consumed across China, India, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Culturally significant in Chinese medicine as a cooling food (qīng rè jiě dú) and in Ayurvedic tradition as a tridoshic legume.
common_user_queries: are mung beans good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F09020021), an approved primary regional source, with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175188) and ICMR-NIN 2017. All three sources are in close agreement. GI value of 31 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled whole mung beans. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03316096 and FoodEx2 A037H) are exact matches. Confidence is high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for whole mung beans not separately reported in HPB Foodo F09020021; USDA FDC 175188 reports total fat only at this level of detail for this entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo F09020021 for this food code. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based legume contains no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no significant vitamin D in dried legumes). GI of 31 applies to the boiled/cooked form; the per_100g nutritional data is for the raw dried form. Glycemic load per serving calculated on 50 g dry serving basis: (31 × 62.6 × 0.50) / 100 = 9.7. ICMR-NIN is listed as a cross-reference source; HPB Foodo is the primary approved source used.
